revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan. Head temple of Honganji-ha school}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nishi Hongan-ji
西本願寺 | native_name = | image = 170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan03s4.jpg | alt = | caption = Amidadō and Goeidō (National Treasures) | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 60 Monzen-machi, Horikawa-dōri Hanaya-chō Kudaru, [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|31.37|N|135|45|5.8|E|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Honganji-ha | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitābha) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple | website = http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/ | founded_by = [[Kōsa|Honganji Kennyo]] | year_completed = 1591 }} {{nihongo|'''Nishi Hongan-ji'''|西本願寺|}} is a {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} [[Buddhist]] temple in the [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It serves as the head temple of the sub-sect [[Honganji-ha]]. It is one of two {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} temple complexes in Kyoto, the other being [[Higashi Hongan-ji]], which is the head temple of the sub-sect [[Otani-ha|Ōtani-ha]]. Established in its current location in 1591, the origin of the temple goes back to the 14th century. Many of its building have survived from the [[Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama]] and early [[Edo period]], making it a great example of the Japanese architecture from the 17th and 18th centuries. A total of seven Nishi Hongan-ji structures have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in three different categories: the [[karamon]], Goei-dō and [[Amitābha|Amida]] hall ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]), the Flying Cloud Pavilion, [[shoin]] and the Black study hall, including the Denrō gallery ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]) and the north [[Noh]] stage ([[National Treasure (Japan)#Miscellaneous structure|miscellaneous structure]]). Nishi Hongan-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]. ==History== The original [[Hongan-ji]] was established as a temple in 1321, on the site of the Ōtani Mausoleum, where [[Shinran]], the founder of the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} ("True Pure Land") was buried. The mausoleum was attended by Shinran's grandson (through daughter Kakushinni), Kakue.{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |title=History of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130404122141/http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |archive-date=2013-04-04 }} Kakue's own son, [[Kakunyo]], became the first chief priest of the Hongan-ji and third [[monshu]] (spiritual leader), and dedicated it to the worship of [[Amitābha]] (''Amida''). The Hongan-ji first gained power and importance in the 15th century, when [[Rennyo]] became its eighth monshu. However, the [[Tendai]] based on [[Mount Hiei]] saw this expansion as a threat and attacked the Hongan-ji three times with their army of [[sōhei]]. [[Rennyo]] fled to [[Yoshizaki-gobō]], where he established a new temple compound. During the [[Sengoku period]], fearing the power of the monks of the Hongan-ji, [[Oda Nobunaga]] tried to destroy it. For ten years, he laid siege to the [[Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] in [[Osaka]], one of the two primary temple fortresses of the sect. In 1580, the abbot of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji, Kennyo, surrendered, while his son Kyōnyo refused to surrender, for which he was publicly disowned.{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123 123-4]|oclc=137242947}} After the death of Nobunaga in 1582 and the ascent of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], Kennyo was rewarded for his opposition to Nobunaga by being granted land in Kyoto, at the site of modern-day Nishi Hongan-ji (西本願寺, "Western Hongan-ji"; sometimes called the ''Honpa Hongan-ji'' 本派本願寺). He was succeeded by his legitimate son, Junnyo, as abbot in 1592. While his brother Kyōnyo re-established the Osaka Hongan-ji in 1596 with local support, owing to his refusal to surrender to Nobunaga earlier. After the death of Hideyoshi in 1598, Kyōnyo openly supported [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], who became ''[[shōgun]]'' in 1602. In reward for his loyalty, Kyōnyo was rewarded with land for a temple in Kyoto to the east of Nishi Honganji, which then became known in 1603 as [[Higashi Honganji]] (東本願寺 "Eastern Hongan-ji"). In 1619 the government recognized the two entities as separate congregations. It is popularly believed, however mistakenly, that the institution was split in two in order to maintain control of the order.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9C%AC%E6%B4%BE%E6%9C%AC%E9%A1%98%E5%AF%BA-632494|title=Hompa honganji|website=Kotobank|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|access-date=2017-10-12|editor=Digital Daijisen}}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124 124-5]|oclc=137242947}} In 1994 Nishi Hongan-ji was listed as a [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]] as one of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] . ==Architecture== Nishi Hongan-ji occupies almost an entire rectangular area bounded by [[Hanayachō-dōri]] (Hanayachō Street) to the north, [[Horikawa-dōri]] (Horikawa Street) to the east,{{Ref|A|A}} [[Shichijō-dōri]] (Shichijō Street) to the south, and [[Ōmiya-dōri]] (Omiya Street) to the west. The main entrance to Nishi Hongan-ji is to the east on Horikawa-dōri. As the name of the temple implies, it is located to the west of Higashi Hongan-ji. Nishi Hongan-ji is older than the latter and has a more integral [[architecture]].Popular Buddhism In Japan: Shin Buddhist Religion & Culture by Esben Andreasen, pp. 11, 38-39, 105 / University of Hawaii Press 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-2028-2}} [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都418.JPG|thumb|left|''Karamon'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都447.JPG|thumb|right|Chao Fu leading his ox away from the water tainted by [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] washing his ears]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都397.JPG|thumb|left|detail of the door, front view]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都439.JPG|thumb|right|Carving of a [[Chinese dragon]]]] ===Karamon=== The {{nihongo|[[karamon]]|唐門}} gate of Nishi Hongan-ji was originally built for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[Fushimi castle]], probably around 1598. After the castle was dismantled in 1623, it was moved to Nishi Hongan-ji in 1632 for a planned visit of [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] to the temple.Kirby, John B. (1962). ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.'' pp. 78. It was last renovated in 2018–2021.{{cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20211120/p2a/00m/0na/024000c|title=Dazzling beauty: Restoration of gate at Kyoto's Nishi Hongwanji temple completed|author=Yoko Minami|date=22 November 2021|publisher=[[The Mainichi]]|access-date=2023-08-16}} It is designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]], one of six such a structures to hold this distinction. It is constructed as a four-legged gate with ''[[karahafu]]'' gables of undulating curves on the front and back.{{cite encyclopedia | title = Karahafu (唐破風) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} It has a roof in the ''[[irimoya]]'' style, a style of [[hip roof]] sloping down on all four sides and integrated on two opposing sides with a [[gable]].{{cite encyclopedia | title = Irimoya-zukuri (入母屋造) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/irimoyazukuri.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} The roof is covered by bark [[Roof shingle|shingles]] made from [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]]. It is also known as the ''Higurashi no Mon'' ("all day gate"),{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/hongwanji/architect.html#ken03 |title=Architecture of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 }} due to the high number and quality of the carving that decorate the gate, including images of flowers, animals and fantastic figures. One of the panels shows the legendary chinese hermit [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] beside a waterfall, "washing from his ear an offensive proposal from the [[Emperor Yao]]". Another one shows a farmer cleaning his ox, "expressing anger at the pollution of the stream". The last two times the gate was opened and visitors were allowed to walk through it were in 1983, during a rite related to the founder of {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Buddhism [[Shinran]], and in 2017, prior to the renovation of the gate in 2018.{{cite web |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2017/05/21/national/social-issues/nishi-honganji-temple-opens-decorative-gate-first-time-decades/#.W6-CHS4iLIU |title=Nishi Honganji Temple opens decorative gate for first time in decades |author= |date=21 May 2017 |publisher=The Japan Times |access-date=2018-09-29 }} It is also used for occasional visits of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]]. [[File:170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06n.jpg|thumb|left|''Goeidō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:170128 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|right|front view of the ''Goeidō'']] ===Goeidō=== The {{nihongo|goeidō|御影堂}} or "Founder's Hall" was rebuilt in 1636, following the destruction of the previous main halls by an earthquake in 1596 and a fire in 1617. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189663 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} The building, single-storied, with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof,{{cite encyclopedia | title = Hongawarabuki (本瓦葺) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hongawarabuki.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} a tile roof composed of flat broad concave tiles and semi-cylindrical convex tiles covering the seams of the former, in the [[irimoya]] style. It measures 62 by 48 metres, with a height on 29 metres. A wooden image of [[Shinran]] is enshrined in the central altar, with portraits of the successive head priests (''monshu'') on display alongside. Major ceremonies conducted at Nishi Hongan-ji are usually conducted at this building. [[File:Nishi Hongwanji Amidado.jpg|thumb|left|''Amidadō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都137.JPG|thumb|right|lateral view of the ''Amidadō'' and the corridor leading to the ''Goeidō'']] ===Amidadō=== The {{nihongo|amidadō|阿弥陀堂}} or "Amida Hall" was rebuilt in 1760 as the temple's main worship hall. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/175062 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} It is a single-storied building with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof in the [[irimoya]] style, measuring 45 by 42 metres, with a height on 25 metres. It houses a sculpture of [[Amida Buddha]] surrounded by the portraits of six of the seven Pure Land Masters in the central altar, and images of [[Shinran]]'s master [[Honen]] and [[Prince Shotoku]]. Due to renovations, the interior of the ''Amidado'' was not accessible to the public until February 2019, when the front half of the hall was opened. The back half was closed until the end of March 2022, when the restoration work concluded. == See also == * [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (miscellaneous structures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Footnotes== :A.{{Note|A}}The defunct ''[[hanamachi]]'' courtesan's district of [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] is located directly to the west of the north side of Nishi Hongan-ji along Hanayachō-dōri. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== {{Commons category|Nishi Hongwanji|Nishi Hongan-ji}} {{Refbegin}} * Kirby, John B. (1962). [https://archive.org/details/fromcastletoteah00kirb ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.''] Tokyo: Tuttle Publishing. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/512972 OCLC 512972] * Rogers, Minor L and Ann T. (1990). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140609131326/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2415 The Honganji: Guardian of the state (1868–1945)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 17 (1), 3-28 * Alicia, Matsunaga (1993). ''Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods).'' Buddhist Books International. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/137242947 OCLC 137242947] {{Refend}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:1270s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1272 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Pure Land temples]] [[Category:Shinshū Honganji-ha temples|*]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Monzeki]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan. Head temple of Honganji-ha school}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nishi Hongan-ji
西本願寺 | native_name = | image = 170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan03s4.jpg | alt = | caption = Amidadō and Goeidō (National Treasures) | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 60 Monzen-machi, Horikawa-dōri Hanaya-chō Kudaru, [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|31.37|N|135|45|5.8|E|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Honganji-ha | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitābha) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple | website = http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/ | founded_by = [[Kōsa|Honganji Kennyo]] | year_completed = 1591 }} {{nihongo|'''Nishi Hongan-ji'''|西本願寺|}} is a {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} [[Buddhist]] temple in the [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It serves as the head temple of the sub-sect [[Honganji-ha]]. It is one of two {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} temple complexes in Kyoto, the other being [[Higashi Hongan-ji]], which is the head temple of the sub-sect [[Otani-ha|Ōtani-ha]]. Established in its current location in 1591, the origin of the temple goes back to the 14th century. Many of its building have survived from the [[Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama]] and early [[Edo period]], making it a great example of the Japanese architecture from the 17th and 18th centuries. A total of seven Nishi Hongan-ji structures have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in three different categories: the [[karamon]], Goei-dō and [[Amitābha|Amida]] hall ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]), the Flying Cloud Pavilion, [[shoin]] and the Black study hall, including the Denrō gallery ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]) and the north [[Noh]] stage ([[National Treasure (Japan)#Miscellaneous structure|miscellaneous structure]]). Nishi Hongan-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]. ==History== The original [[Hongan-ji]] was established as a temple in 1321, on the site of the Ōtani Mausoleum, where [[Shinran]], the founder of the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} ("True Pure Land") was buried. The mausoleum was attended by Shinran's grandson (through daughter Kakushinni), Kakue.{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |title=History of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130404122141/http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |archive-date=2013-04-04 }} Kakue's own son, [[Kakunyo]], became the first chief priest of the Hongan-ji and third [[monshu]] (spiritual leader), and dedicated it to the worship of [[Amitābha]] (''Amida''). The Hongan-ji first gained power and importance in the 15th century, when [[Rennyo]] became its eighth monshu. However, the [[Tendai]] based on [[Mount Hiei]] saw this expansion as a threat and attacked the Hongan-ji three times with their army of [[sōhei]]. [[Rennyo]] fled to [[Yoshizaki-gobō]], where he established a new temple compound. During the [[Sengoku period]], fearing the power of the monks of the Hongan-ji, [[Oda Nobunaga]] tried to destroy it. For ten years, he laid siege to the [[Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] in [[Osaka]], one of the two primary temple fortresses of the sect. In 1580, the abbot of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji, Kennyo, surrendered, while his son Kyōnyo refused to surrender, for which he was publicly disowned.{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123 123-4]|oclc=137242947}} After the death of Nobunaga in 1582 and the ascent of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], Kennyo was rewarded for his opposition to Nobunaga by being granted land in Kyoto, at the site of modern-day Nishi Hongan-ji (西本願寺, "Western Hongan-ji"; sometimes called the ''Honpa Hongan-ji'' 本派本願寺). He was succeeded by his legitimate son, Junnyo, as abbot in 1592. While his brother Kyōnyo re-established the Osaka Hongan-ji in 1596 with local support, owing to his refusal to surrender to Nobunaga earlier. After the death of Hideyoshi in 1598, Kyōnyo openly supported [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], who became ''[[shōgun]]'' in 1602. In reward for his loyalty, Kyōnyo was rewarded with land for a temple in Kyoto to the east of Nishi Honganji, which then became known in 1603 as [[Higashi Honganji]] (東本願寺 "Eastern Hongan-ji"). In 1619 the government recognized the two entities as separate congregations. It is popularly believed, however mistakenly, that the institution was split in two in order to maintain control of the order.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9C%AC%E6%B4%BE%E6%9C%AC%E9%A1%98%E5%AF%BA-632494|title=Hompa honganji|website=Kotobank|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|access-date=2017-10-12|editor=Digital Daijisen}}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124 124-5]|oclc=137242947}} In 1994 Nishi Hongan-ji was listed as a [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]] as one of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] . ==Architecture== Nishi Hongan-ji occupies almost an entire rectangular area bounded by [[Hanayachō-dōri]] (Hanayachō Street) to the north, [[Horikawa-dōri]] (Horikawa Street) to the east,{{Ref|A|A}} [[Shichijō-dōri]] (Shichijō Street) to the south, and [[Ōmiya-dōri]] (Omiya Street) to the west. The main entrance to Nishi Hongan-ji is to the east on Horikawa-dōri. As the name of the temple implies, it is located to the west of Higashi Hongan-ji. Nishi Hongan-ji is older than the latter and has a more integral [[architecture]].Popular Buddhism In Japan: Shin Buddhist Religion & Culture by Esben Andreasen, pp. 11, 38-39, 105 / University of Hawaii Press 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-2028-2}} [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都418.JPG|thumb|left|''Karamon'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都447.JPG|thumb|right|Chao Fu leading his ox away from the water tainted by [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] washing his ears]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都397.JPG|thumb|left|detail of the door, front view]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都439.JPG|thumb|right|Carving of a [[Chinese dragon]]]] ===Karamon=== The {{nihongo|[[karamon]]|唐門}} gate of Nishi Hongan-ji was originally built for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[Fushimi castle]], probably around 1598. After the castle was dismantled in 1623, it was moved to Nishi Hongan-ji in 1632 for a planned visit of [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] to the temple.Kirby, John B. (1962). ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.'' pp. 78. It was last renovated in 2018–2021.{{cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20211120/p2a/00m/0na/024000c|title=Dazzling beauty: Restoration of gate at Kyoto's Nishi Hongwanji temple completed|author=Yoko Minami|date=22 November 2021|publisher=[[The Mainichi]]|access-date=2023-08-16}} It is designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]], one of six such a structures to hold this distinction. It is constructed as a four-legged gate with ''[[karahafu]]'' gables of undulating curves on the front and back.{{cite encyclopedia | title = Karahafu (唐破風) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} It has a roof in the ''[[irimoya]]'' style, a style of [[hip roof]] sloping down on all four sides and integrated on two opposing sides with a [[gable]].{{cite encyclopedia | title = Irimoya-zukuri (入母屋造) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/irimoyazukuri.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} The roof is covered by bark [[Roof shingle|shingles]] made from [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]]. It is also known as the ''Higurashi no Mon'' ("all day gate"),{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/hongwanji/architect.html#ken03 |title=Architecture of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 }} due to the high number and quality of the carving that decorate the gate, including images of flowers, animals and fantastic figures. One of the panels shows the legendary chinese hermit [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] beside a waterfall, "washing from his ear an offensive proposal from the [[Emperor Yao]]". Another one shows a farmer cleaning his ox, "expressing anger at the pollution of the stream". The last two times the gate was opened and visitors were allowed to walk through it were in 1983, during a rite related to the founder of {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Buddhism [[Shinran]], and in 2017, prior to the renovation of the gate in 2018.{{cite web |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2017/05/21/national/social-issues/nishi-honganji-temple-opens-decorative-gate-first-time-decades/#.W6-CHS4iLIU |title=Nishi Honganji Temple opens decorative gate for first time in decades |author= |date=21 May 2017 |publisher=The Japan Times |access-date=2018-09-29 }} It is also used for occasional visits of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]]. [[File:170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06n.jpg|thumb|left|''Goeidō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:170128 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|right|front view of the ''Goeidō'']] ===Goeidō=== The {{nihongo|goeidō|御影堂}} or "Founder's Hall" was rebuilt in 1636, following the destruction of the previous main halls by an earthquake in 1596 and a fire in 1617. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189663 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} The building, single-storied, with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof,{{cite encyclopedia | title = Hongawarabuki (本瓦葺) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hongawarabuki.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} a tile roof composed of flat broad concave tiles and semi-cylindrical convex tiles covering the seams of the former, in the [[irimoya]] style. It measures 62 by 48 metres, with a height on 29 metres. A wooden image of [[Shinran]] is enshrined in the central altar, with portraits of the successive head priests (''monshu'') on display alongside. Major ceremonies conducted at Nishi Hongan-ji are usually conducted at this building. [[File:Nishi Hongwanji Amidado.jpg|thumb|left|''Amidadō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都137.JPG|thumb|right|lateral view of the ''Amidadō'' and the corridor leading to the ''Goeidō'']] ===Amidadō=== The {{nihongo|amidadō|阿弥陀堂}} or "Amida Hall" was rebuilt in 1760 as the temple's main worship hall. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/175062 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} It is a single-storied building with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof in the [[irimoya]] style, measuring 45 by 42 metres, with a height on 25 metres. It houses a sculpture of [[Amida Buddha]] surrounded by the portraits of six of the seven Pure Land Masters in the central altar, and images of [[Shinran]]'s master [[Honen]] and [[Prince Shotoku]]. Due to renovations, the interior of the ''Amidado'' was not accessible to the public until February 2019, when the front half of the hall was opened. The back half was closed until the end of March 2022, when the restoration work concluded. == See also == * [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (miscellaneous structures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Footnotes== :A.{{Note|A}}The defunct ''[[hanamachi]]'' courtesan's district of [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] is located directly to the west of the north side of Nishi Hongan-ji along Hanayachō-dōri. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== {{Commons category|Nishi Hongwanji|Nishi Hongan-ji}} {{Refbegin}} * Kirby, John B. (1962). [https://archive.org/details/fromcastletoteah00kirb ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.''] Tokyo: Tuttle Publishing. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/512972 OCLC 512972] * Rogers, Minor L and Ann T. (1990). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140609131326/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2415 The Honganji: Guardian of the state (1868–1945)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 17 (1), 3-28 * Alicia, Matsunaga (1993). ''Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods).'' Buddhist Books International. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/137242947 OCLC 137242947] {{Refend}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:1270s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1272 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Pure Land temples]] [[Category:Shinshū Honganji-ha temples|*]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Monzeki]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan. Head temple of Honganji-ha school}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nishi Hongan-ji
西本願寺 | native_name = | image = 170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan03s4.jpg | alt = | caption = Amidadō and Goeidō (National Treasures) | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 60 Monzen-machi, Horikawa-dōri Hanaya-chō Kudaru, [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|31.37|N|135|45|5.8|E|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Honganji-ha | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitābha) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple | website = http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/ | founded_by = [[Kōsa|Honganji Kennyo]] | year_completed = 1591 }} {{nihongo|'''Nishi Hongan-ji'''|西本願寺|}} is a {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} [[Buddhist]] temple in the [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It serves as the head temple of the sub-sect [[Honganji-ha]]. It is one of two {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} temple complexes in Kyoto, the other being [[Higashi Hongan-ji]], which is the head temple of the sub-sect [[Otani-ha|Ōtani-ha]]. Established in its current location in 1591, the origin of the temple goes back to the 14th century. Many of its building have survived from the [[Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama]] and early [[Edo period]], making it a great example of the Japanese architecture from the 17th and 18th centuries. A total of seven Nishi Hongan-ji structures have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in three different categories: the [[karamon]], Goei-dō and [[Amitābha|Amida]] hall ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]), the Flying Cloud Pavilion, [[shoin]] and the Black study hall, including the Denrō gallery ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]) and the north [[Noh]] stage ([[National Treasure (Japan)#Miscellaneous structure|miscellaneous structure]]). Nishi Hongan-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]. ==History== The original [[Hongan-ji]] was established as a temple in 1321, on the site of the Ōtani Mausoleum, where [[Shinran]], the founder of the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} ("True Pure Land") was buried. The mausoleum was attended by Shinran's grandson (through daughter Kakushinni), Kakue.{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |title=History of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130404122141/http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |archive-date=2013-04-04 }} Kakue's own son, [[Kakunyo]], became the first chief priest of the Hongan-ji and third [[monshu]] (spiritual leader), and dedicated it to the worship of [[Amitābha]] (''Amida''). The Hongan-ji first gained power and importance in the 15th century, when [[Rennyo]] became its eighth monshu. However, the [[Tendai]] based on [[Mount Hiei]] saw this expansion as a threat and attacked the Hongan-ji three times with their army of [[sōhei]]. [[Rennyo]] fled to [[Yoshizaki-gobō]], where he established a new temple compound. During the [[Sengoku period]], fearing the power of the monks of the Hongan-ji, [[Oda Nobunaga]] tried to destroy it. For ten years, he laid siege to the [[Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] in [[Osaka]], one of the two primary temple fortresses of the sect. In 1580, the abbot of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji, Kennyo, surrendered, while his son Kyōnyo refused to surrender, for which he was publicly disowned.{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123 123-4]|oclc=137242947}} After the death of Nobunaga in 1582 and the ascent of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], Kennyo was rewarded for his opposition to Nobunaga by being granted land in Kyoto, at the site of modern-day Nishi Hongan-ji (西本願寺, "Western Hongan-ji"; sometimes called the ''Honpa Hongan-ji'' 本派本願寺). He was succeeded by his legitimate son, Junnyo, as abbot in 1592. While his brother Kyōnyo re-established the Osaka Hongan-ji in 1596 with local support, owing to his refusal to surrender to Nobunaga earlier. After the death of Hideyoshi in 1598, Kyōnyo openly supported [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], who became ''[[shōgun]]'' in 1602. In reward for his loyalty, Kyōnyo was rewarded with land for a temple in Kyoto to the east of Nishi Honganji, which then became known in 1603 as [[Higashi Honganji]] (東本願寺 "Eastern Hongan-ji"). In 1619 the government recognized the two entities as separate congregations. It is popularly believed, however mistakenly, that the institution was split in two in order to maintain control of the order.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9C%AC%E6%B4%BE%E6%9C%AC%E9%A1%98%E5%AF%BA-632494|title=Hompa honganji|website=Kotobank|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|access-date=2017-10-12|editor=Digital Daijisen}}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124 124-5]|oclc=137242947}} In 1994 Nishi Hongan-ji was listed as a [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]] as one of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] . ==Architecture== Nishi Hongan-ji occupies almost an entire rectangular area bounded by [[Hanayachō-dōri]] (Hanayachō Street) to the north, [[Horikawa-dōri]] (Horikawa Street) to the east,{{Ref|A|A}} [[Shichijō-dōri]] (Shichijō Street) to the south, and [[Ōmiya-dōri]] (Omiya Street) to the west. The main entrance to Nishi Hongan-ji is to the east on Horikawa-dōri. As the name of the temple implies, it is located to the west of Higashi Hongan-ji. Nishi Hongan-ji is older than the latter and has a more integral [[architecture]].Popular Buddhism In Japan: Shin Buddhist Religion & Culture by Esben Andreasen, pp. 11, 38-39, 105 / University of Hawaii Press 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-2028-2}} [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都418.JPG|thumb|left|''Karamon'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都447.JPG|thumb|right|Chao Fu leading his ox away from the water tainted by [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] washing his ears]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都397.JPG|thumb|left|detail of the door, front view]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都439.JPG|thumb|right|Carving of a [[Chinese dragon]]]] ===Karamon=== The {{nihongo|[[karamon]]|唐門}} gate of Nishi Hongan-ji was originally built for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[Fushimi castle]], probably around 1598. After the castle was dismantled in 1623, it was moved to Nishi Hongan-ji in 1632 for a planned visit of [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] to the temple.Kirby, John B. (1962). ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.'' pp. 78. It was last renovated in 2018–2021.{{cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20211120/p2a/00m/0na/024000c|title=Dazzling beauty: Restoration of gate at Kyoto's Nishi Hongwanji temple completed|author=Yoko Minami|date=22 November 2021|publisher=[[The Mainichi]]|access-date=2023-08-16}} It is designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]], one of six such a structures to hold this distinction. It is constructed as a four-legged gate with ''[[karahafu]]'' gables of undulating curves on the front and back.{{cite encyclopedia | title = Karahafu (唐破風) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} It has a roof in the ''[[irimoya]]'' style, a style of [[hip roof]] sloping down on all four sides and integrated on two opposing sides with a [[gable]].{{cite encyclopedia | title = Irimoya-zukuri (入母屋造) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/irimoyazukuri.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} The roof is covered by bark [[Roof shingle|shingles]] made from [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]]. It is also known as the ''Higurashi no Mon'' ("all day gate"),{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/hongwanji/architect.html#ken03 |title=Architecture of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 }} due to the high number and quality of the carving that decorate the gate, including images of flowers, animals and fantastic figures. One of the panels shows the legendary chinese hermit [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] beside a waterfall, "washing from his ear an offensive proposal from the [[Emperor Yao]]". Another one shows a farmer cleaning his ox, "expressing anger at the pollution of the stream". The last two times the gate was opened and visitors were allowed to walk through it were in 1983, during a rite related to the founder of {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Buddhism [[Shinran]], and in 2017, prior to the renovation of the gate in 2018.{{cite web |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2017/05/21/national/social-issues/nishi-honganji-temple-opens-decorative-gate-first-time-decades/#.W6-CHS4iLIU |title=Nishi Honganji Temple opens decorative gate for first time in decades |author= |date=21 May 2017 |publisher=The Japan Times |access-date=2018-09-29 }} It is also used for occasional visits of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]]. [[File:170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06n.jpg|thumb|left|''Goeidō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:170128 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|right|front view of the ''Goeidō'']] ===Goeidō=== The {{nihongo|goeidō|御影堂}} or "Founder's Hall" was rebuilt in 1636, following the destruction of the previous main halls by an earthquake in 1596 and a fire in 1617. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189663 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} The building, single-storied, with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof,{{cite encyclopedia | title = Hongawarabuki (本瓦葺) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hongawarabuki.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} a tile roof composed of flat broad concave tiles and semi-cylindrical convex tiles covering the seams of the former, in the [[irimoya]] style. It measures 62 by 48 metres, with a height on 29 metres. A wooden image of [[Shinran]] is enshrined in the central altar, with portraits of the successive head priests (''monshu'') on display alongside. Major ceremonies conducted at Nishi Hongan-ji are usually conducted at this building. [[File:Nishi Hongwanji Amidado.jpg|thumb|left|''Amidadō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都137.JPG|thumb|right|lateral view of the ''Amidadō'' and the corridor leading to the ''Goeidō'']] ===Amidadō=== The {{nihongo|amidadō|阿弥陀堂}} or "Amida Hall" was rebuilt in 1760 as the temple's main worship hall. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/175062 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} It is a single-storied building with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof in the [[irimoya]] style, measuring 45 by 42 metres, with a height on 25 metres. It houses a sculpture of [[Amida Buddha]] surrounded by the portraits of six of the seven Pure Land Masters in the central altar, and images of [[Shinran]]'s master [[Honen]] and [[Prince Shotoku]]. Due to renovations, the interior of the ''Amidado'' was not accessible to the public until February 2019, when the front half of the hall was opened. The back half was closed until the end of March 2022, when the restoration work concluded. == See also == * [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (miscellaneous structures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Footnotes== :A.{{Note|A}}The defunct ''[[hanamachi]]'' courtesan's district of [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] is located directly to the west of the north side of Nishi Hongan-ji along Hanayachō-dōri. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== {{Commons category|Nishi Hongwanji|Nishi Hongan-ji}} {{Refbegin}} * Kirby, John B. (1962). [https://archive.org/details/fromcastletoteah00kirb ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.''] Tokyo: Tuttle Publishing. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/512972 OCLC 512972] * Rogers, Minor L and Ann T. (1990). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140609131326/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2415 The Honganji: Guardian of the state (1868–1945)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 17 (1), 3-28 * Alicia, Matsunaga (1993). ''Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods).'' Buddhist Books International. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/137242947 OCLC 137242947] {{Refend}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:1270s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1272 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Pure Land temples]] [[Category:Shinshū Honganji-ha temples|*]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Monzeki]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan. Head temple of Honganji-ha school}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nishi Hongan-ji
西本願寺 | native_name = | image = 170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan03s4.jpg | alt = | caption = Amidadō and Goeidō (National Treasures) | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 60 Monzen-machi, Horikawa-dōri Hanaya-chō Kudaru, [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|31.37|N|135|45|5.8|E|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Honganji-ha | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitābha) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple | website = http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/ | founded_by = [[Kōsa|Honganji Kennyo]] | year_completed = 1591 }} {{nihongo|'''Nishi Hongan-ji'''|西本願寺|}} is a {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} [[Buddhist]] temple in the [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It serves as the head temple of the sub-sect [[Honganji-ha]]. It is one of two {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} temple complexes in Kyoto, the other being [[Higashi Hongan-ji]], which is the head temple of the sub-sect [[Otani-ha|Ōtani-ha]]. Established in its current location in 1591, the origin of the temple goes back to the 14th century. Many of its building have survived from the [[Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama]] and early [[Edo period]], making it a great example of the Japanese architecture from the 17th and 18th centuries. A total of seven Nishi Hongan-ji structures have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in three different categories: the [[karamon]], Goei-dō and [[Amitābha|Amida]] hall ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]), the Flying Cloud Pavilion, [[shoin]] and the Black study hall, including the Denrō gallery ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]) and the north [[Noh]] stage ([[National Treasure (Japan)#Miscellaneous structure|miscellaneous structure]]). Nishi Hongan-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]. ==History== The original [[Hongan-ji]] was established as a temple in 1321, on the site of the Ōtani Mausoleum, where [[Shinran]], the founder of the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} ("True Pure Land") was buried. The mausoleum was attended by Shinran's grandson (through daughter Kakushinni), Kakue.{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |title=History of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130404122141/http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |archive-date=2013-04-04 }} Kakue's own son, [[Kakunyo]], became the first chief priest of the Hongan-ji and third [[monshu]] (spiritual leader), and dedicated it to the worship of [[Amitābha]] (''Amida''). The Hongan-ji first gained power and importance in the 15th century, when [[Rennyo]] became its eighth monshu. However, the [[Tendai]] based on [[Mount Hiei]] saw this expansion as a threat and attacked the Hongan-ji three times with their army of [[sōhei]]. [[Rennyo]] fled to [[Yoshizaki-gobō]], where he established a new temple compound. During the [[Sengoku period]], fearing the power of the monks of the Hongan-ji, [[Oda Nobunaga]] tried to destroy it. For ten years, he laid siege to the [[Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] in [[Osaka]], one of the two primary temple fortresses of the sect. In 1580, the abbot of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji, Kennyo, surrendered, while his son Kyōnyo refused to surrender, for which he was publicly disowned.{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123 123-4]|oclc=137242947}} After the death of Nobunaga in 1582 and the ascent of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], Kennyo was rewarded for his opposition to Nobunaga by being granted land in Kyoto, at the site of modern-day Nishi Hongan-ji (西本願寺, "Western Hongan-ji"; sometimes called the ''Honpa Hongan-ji'' 本派本願寺). He was succeeded by his legitimate son, Junnyo, as abbot in 1592. While his brother Kyōnyo re-established the Osaka Hongan-ji in 1596 with local support, owing to his refusal to surrender to Nobunaga earlier. After the death of Hideyoshi in 1598, Kyōnyo openly supported [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], who became ''[[shōgun]]'' in 1602. In reward for his loyalty, Kyōnyo was rewarded with land for a temple in Kyoto to the east of Nishi Honganji, which then became known in 1603 as [[Higashi Honganji]] (東本願寺 "Eastern Hongan-ji"). In 1619 the government recognized the two entities as separate congregations. It is popularly believed, however mistakenly, that the institution was split in two in order to maintain control of the order.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9C%AC%E6%B4%BE%E6%9C%AC%E9%A1%98%E5%AF%BA-632494|title=Hompa honganji|website=Kotobank|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|access-date=2017-10-12|editor=Digital Daijisen}}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124 124-5]|oclc=137242947}} In 1994 Nishi Hongan-ji was listed as a [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]] as one of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] . ==Architecture== Nishi Hongan-ji occupies almost an entire rectangular area bounded by [[Hanayachō-dōri]] (Hanayachō Street) to the north, [[Horikawa-dōri]] (Horikawa Street) to the east,{{Ref|A|A}} [[Shichijō-dōri]] (Shichijō Street) to the south, and [[Ōmiya-dōri]] (Omiya Street) to the west. The main entrance to Nishi Hongan-ji is to the east on Horikawa-dōri. As the name of the temple implies, it is located to the west of Higashi Hongan-ji. Nishi Hongan-ji is older than the latter and has a more integral [[architecture]].Popular Buddhism In Japan: Shin Buddhist Religion & Culture by Esben Andreasen, pp. 11, 38-39, 105 / University of Hawaii Press 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-2028-2}} [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都418.JPG|thumb|left|''Karamon'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都447.JPG|thumb|right|Chao Fu leading his ox away from the water tainted by [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] washing his ears]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都397.JPG|thumb|left|detail of the door, front view]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都439.JPG|thumb|right|Carving of a [[Chinese dragon]]]] ===Karamon=== The {{nihongo|[[karamon]]|唐門}} gate of Nishi Hongan-ji was originally built for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[Fushimi castle]], probably around 1598. After the castle was dismantled in 1623, it was moved to Nishi Hongan-ji in 1632 for a planned visit of [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] to the temple.Kirby, John B. (1962). ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.'' pp. 78. It was last renovated in 2018–2021.{{cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20211120/p2a/00m/0na/024000c|title=Dazzling beauty: Restoration of gate at Kyoto's Nishi Hongwanji temple completed|author=Yoko Minami|date=22 November 2021|publisher=[[The Mainichi]]|access-date=2023-08-16}} It is designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]], one of six such a structures to hold this distinction. It is constructed as a four-legged gate with ''[[karahafu]]'' gables of undulating curves on the front and back.{{cite encyclopedia | title = Karahafu (唐破風) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} It has a roof in the ''[[irimoya]]'' style, a style of [[hip roof]] sloping down on all four sides and integrated on two opposing sides with a [[gable]].{{cite encyclopedia | title = Irimoya-zukuri (入母屋造) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/irimoyazukuri.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} The roof is covered by bark [[Roof shingle|shingles]] made from [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]]. It is also known as the ''Higurashi no Mon'' ("all day gate"),{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/hongwanji/architect.html#ken03 |title=Architecture of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 }} due to the high number and quality of the carving that decorate the gate, including images of flowers, animals and fantastic figures. One of the panels shows the legendary chinese hermit [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] beside a waterfall, "washing from his ear an offensive proposal from the [[Emperor Yao]]". Another one shows a farmer cleaning his ox, "expressing anger at the pollution of the stream". The last two times the gate was opened and visitors were allowed to walk through it were in 1983, during a rite related to the founder of {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Buddhism [[Shinran]], and in 2017, prior to the renovation of the gate in 2018.{{cite web |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2017/05/21/national/social-issues/nishi-honganji-temple-opens-decorative-gate-first-time-decades/#.W6-CHS4iLIU |title=Nishi Honganji Temple opens decorative gate for first time in decades |author= |date=21 May 2017 |publisher=The Japan Times |access-date=2018-09-29 }} It is also used for occasional visits of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]]. [[File:170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06n.jpg|thumb|left|''Goeidō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:170128 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|right|front view of the ''Goeidō'']] ===Goeidō=== The {{nihongo|goeidō|御影堂}} or "Founder's Hall" was rebuilt in 1636, following the destruction of the previous main halls by an earthquake in 1596 and a fire in 1617. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189663 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} The building, single-storied, with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof,{{cite encyclopedia | title = Hongawarabuki (本瓦葺) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hongawarabuki.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} a tile roof composed of flat broad concave tiles and semi-cylindrical convex tiles covering the seams of the former, in the [[irimoya]] style. It measures 62 by 48 metres, with a height on 29 metres. A wooden image of [[Shinran]] is enshrined in the central altar, with portraits of the successive head priests (''monshu'') on display alongside. Major ceremonies conducted at Nishi Hongan-ji are usually conducted at this building. [[File:Nishi Hongwanji Amidado.jpg|thumb|left|''Amidadō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都137.JPG|thumb|right|lateral view of the ''Amidadō'' and the corridor leading to the ''Goeidō'']] ===Amidadō=== The {{nihongo|amidadō|阿弥陀堂}} or "Amida Hall" was rebuilt in 1760 as the temple's main worship hall. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/175062 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} It is a single-storied building with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof in the [[irimoya]] style, measuring 45 by 42 metres, with a height on 25 metres. It houses a sculpture of [[Amida Buddha]] surrounded by the portraits of six of the seven Pure Land Masters in the central altar, and images of [[Shinran]]'s master [[Honen]] and [[Prince Shotoku]]. Due to renovations, the interior of the ''Amidado'' was not accessible to the public until February 2019, when the front half of the hall was opened. The back half was closed until the end of March 2022, when the restoration work concluded. == See also == * [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (miscellaneous structures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Footnotes== :A.{{Note|A}}The defunct ''[[hanamachi]]'' courtesan's district of [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] is located directly to the west of the north side of Nishi Hongan-ji along Hanayachō-dōri. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== {{Commons category|Nishi Hongwanji|Nishi Hongan-ji}} {{Refbegin}} * Kirby, John B. (1962). [https://archive.org/details/fromcastletoteah00kirb ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.''] Tokyo: Tuttle Publishing. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/512972 OCLC 512972] * Rogers, Minor L and Ann T. (1990). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140609131326/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2415 The Honganji: Guardian of the state (1868–1945)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 17 (1), 3-28 * Alicia, Matsunaga (1993). ''Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods).'' Buddhist Books International. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/137242947 OCLC 137242947] {{Refend}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:1270s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1272 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Pure Land temples]] [[Category:Shinshū Honganji-ha temples|*]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Monzeki]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan. Head temple of Honganji-ha school}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nishi Hongan-ji
西本願寺 | native_name = | image = 170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan03s4.jpg | alt = | caption = Amidadō and Goeidō (National Treasures) | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 60 Monzen-machi, Horikawa-dōri Hanaya-chō Kudaru, [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|59|31.37|N|135|45|5.8|E|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Honganji-ha | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitābha) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple | website = http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/ | founded_by = [[Kōsa|Honganji Kennyo]] | year_completed = 1591 }} {{nihongo|'''Nishi Hongan-ji'''|西本願寺|}} is a {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} [[Buddhist]] temple in the [[Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|Shimogyō]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It serves as the head temple of the sub-sect [[Honganji-ha]]. It is one of two {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} temple complexes in Kyoto, the other being [[Higashi Hongan-ji]], which is the head temple of the sub-sect [[Otani-ha|Ōtani-ha]]. Established in its current location in 1591, the origin of the temple goes back to the 14th century. Many of its building have survived from the [[Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama]] and early [[Edo period]], making it a great example of the Japanese architecture from the 17th and 18th centuries. A total of seven Nishi Hongan-ji structures have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in three different categories: the [[karamon]], Goei-dō and [[Amitābha|Amida]] hall ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]), the Flying Cloud Pavilion, [[shoin]] and the Black study hall, including the Denrō gallery ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]) and the north [[Noh]] stage ([[National Treasure (Japan)#Miscellaneous structure|miscellaneous structure]]). Nishi Hongan-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]. ==History== The original [[Hongan-ji]] was established as a temple in 1321, on the site of the Ōtani Mausoleum, where [[Shinran]], the founder of the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} ("True Pure Land") was buried. The mausoleum was attended by Shinran's grandson (through daughter Kakushinni), Kakue.{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |title=History of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130404122141/http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/history.html |archive-date=2013-04-04 }} Kakue's own son, [[Kakunyo]], became the first chief priest of the Hongan-ji and third [[monshu]] (spiritual leader), and dedicated it to the worship of [[Amitābha]] (''Amida''). The Hongan-ji first gained power and importance in the 15th century, when [[Rennyo]] became its eighth monshu. However, the [[Tendai]] based on [[Mount Hiei]] saw this expansion as a threat and attacked the Hongan-ji three times with their army of [[sōhei]]. [[Rennyo]] fled to [[Yoshizaki-gobō]], where he established a new temple compound. During the [[Sengoku period]], fearing the power of the monks of the Hongan-ji, [[Oda Nobunaga]] tried to destroy it. For ten years, he laid siege to the [[Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] in [[Osaka]], one of the two primary temple fortresses of the sect. In 1580, the abbot of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji, Kennyo, surrendered, while his son Kyōnyo refused to surrender, for which he was publicly disowned.{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/123 123-4]|oclc=137242947}} After the death of Nobunaga in 1582 and the ascent of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], Kennyo was rewarded for his opposition to Nobunaga by being granted land in Kyoto, at the site of modern-day Nishi Hongan-ji (西本願寺, "Western Hongan-ji"; sometimes called the ''Honpa Hongan-ji'' 本派本願寺). He was succeeded by his legitimate son, Junnyo, as abbot in 1592. While his brother Kyōnyo re-established the Osaka Hongan-ji in 1596 with local support, owing to his refusal to surrender to Nobunaga earlier. After the death of Hideyoshi in 1598, Kyōnyo openly supported [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]], who became ''[[shōgun]]'' in 1602. In reward for his loyalty, Kyōnyo was rewarded with land for a temple in Kyoto to the east of Nishi Honganji, which then became known in 1603 as [[Higashi Honganji]] (東本願寺 "Eastern Hongan-ji"). In 1619 the government recognized the two entities as separate congregations. It is popularly believed, however mistakenly, that the institution was split in two in order to maintain control of the order.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%9C%AC%E6%B4%BE%E6%9C%AC%E9%A1%98%E5%AF%BA-632494|title=Hompa honganji|website=Kotobank|publisher=[[Asahi Shimbun]]|access-date=2017-10-12|editor=Digital Daijisen}}{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124|title=Foundation of Japanese Buddhism. Vol. II, The mass movement (Kamakura & Muromachi periods)|last=Alicia.|first=Matsunaga|date=1993|publisher=Buddhist Books International|others=Matsunaga, Daigan.|isbn=0914910280|location=Los Angeles|pages=[https://archive.org/details/foundationofjapa0000mats/page/124 124-5]|oclc=137242947}} In 1994 Nishi Hongan-ji was listed as a [[UNESCO World Heritage Site]] as one of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] . ==Architecture== Nishi Hongan-ji occupies almost an entire rectangular area bounded by [[Hanayachō-dōri]] (Hanayachō Street) to the north, [[Horikawa-dōri]] (Horikawa Street) to the east,{{Ref|A|A}} [[Shichijō-dōri]] (Shichijō Street) to the south, and [[Ōmiya-dōri]] (Omiya Street) to the west. The main entrance to Nishi Hongan-ji is to the east on Horikawa-dōri. As the name of the temple implies, it is located to the west of Higashi Hongan-ji. Nishi Hongan-ji is older than the latter and has a more integral [[architecture]].Popular Buddhism In Japan: Shin Buddhist Religion & Culture by Esben Andreasen, pp. 11, 38-39, 105 / University of Hawaii Press 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-2028-2}} [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都418.JPG|thumb|left|''Karamon'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都447.JPG|thumb|right|Chao Fu leading his ox away from the water tainted by [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] washing his ears]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都397.JPG|thumb|left|detail of the door, front view]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都439.JPG|thumb|right|Carving of a [[Chinese dragon]]]] ===Karamon=== The {{nihongo|[[karamon]]|唐門}} gate of Nishi Hongan-ji was originally built for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[Fushimi castle]], probably around 1598. After the castle was dismantled in 1623, it was moved to Nishi Hongan-ji in 1632 for a planned visit of [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] to the temple.Kirby, John B. (1962). ''From Castle to Teahouse: Japanese Architecture of the Momoyama Period.'' pp. 78. It was last renovated in 2018–2021.{{cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20211120/p2a/00m/0na/024000c|title=Dazzling beauty: Restoration of gate at Kyoto's Nishi Hongwanji temple completed|author=Yoko Minami|date=22 November 2021|publisher=[[The Mainichi]]|access-date=2023-08-16}} It is designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]], one of six such a structures to hold this distinction. It is constructed as a four-legged gate with ''[[karahafu]]'' gables of undulating curves on the front and back.{{cite encyclopedia | title = Karahafu (唐破風) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/karahafu.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} It has a roof in the ''[[irimoya]]'' style, a style of [[hip roof]] sloping down on all four sides and integrated on two opposing sides with a [[gable]].{{cite encyclopedia | title = Irimoya-zukuri (入母屋造) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/irimoyazukuri.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} The roof is covered by bark [[Roof shingle|shingles]] made from [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]]. It is also known as the ''Higurashi no Mon'' ("all day gate"),{{cite web |url=http://www.hongwanji.or.jp/english/hongwanji/architect.html#ken03 |title=Architecture of Nishi Hongan-ji |author= |publisher=Hongwanji |access-date=2018-09-29 }} due to the high number and quality of the carving that decorate the gate, including images of flowers, animals and fantastic figures. One of the panels shows the legendary chinese hermit [[Xu You (hermit)|Xu You]] beside a waterfall, "washing from his ear an offensive proposal from the [[Emperor Yao]]". Another one shows a farmer cleaning his ox, "expressing anger at the pollution of the stream". The last two times the gate was opened and visitors were allowed to walk through it were in 1983, during a rite related to the founder of {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Jōdo Shinshū]]|italic=no}} Buddhism [[Shinran]], and in 2017, prior to the renovation of the gate in 2018.{{cite web |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2017/05/21/national/social-issues/nishi-honganji-temple-opens-decorative-gate-first-time-decades/#.W6-CHS4iLIU |title=Nishi Honganji Temple opens decorative gate for first time in decades |author= |date=21 May 2017 |publisher=The Japan Times |access-date=2018-09-29 }} It is also used for occasional visits of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]]. [[File:170216 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06n.jpg|thumb|left|''Goeidō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:170128 Nishi Honganji Kyoto Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|right|front view of the ''Goeidō'']] ===Goeidō=== The {{nihongo|goeidō|御影堂}} or "Founder's Hall" was rebuilt in 1636, following the destruction of the previous main halls by an earthquake in 1596 and a fire in 1617. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189663 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} The building, single-storied, with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof,{{cite encyclopedia | title = Hongawarabuki (本瓦葺) | encyclopedia = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | editor-last = Parent | editor-first = Mary Neighbour | place = Tokyo | publisher = Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | date = 2001 | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hongawarabuki.htm | access-date = 2018-09-29 | oclc= 318091406}} a tile roof composed of flat broad concave tiles and semi-cylindrical convex tiles covering the seams of the former, in the [[irimoya]] style. It measures 62 by 48 metres, with a height on 29 metres. A wooden image of [[Shinran]] is enshrined in the central altar, with portraits of the successive head priests (''monshu'') on display alongside. Major ceremonies conducted at Nishi Hongan-ji are usually conducted at this building. [[File:Nishi Hongwanji Amidado.jpg|thumb|left|''Amidadō'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Hongan-ji National Treasure World heritage Kyoto 国宝・世界遺産 本願寺 京都137.JPG|thumb|right|lateral view of the ''Amidadō'' and the corridor leading to the ''Goeidō'']] ===Amidadō=== The {{nihongo|amidadō|阿弥陀堂}} or "Amida Hall" was rebuilt in 1760 as the temple's main worship hall. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 2014.{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/175062 |title=本願寺御影堂 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-20 }} It is a single-storied building with a ''hongawarabuki'' roof in the [[irimoya]] style, measuring 45 by 42 metres, with a height on 25 metres. It houses a sculpture of [[Amida Buddha]] surrounded by the portraits of six of the seven Pure Land Masters in the central altar, and images of [[Shinran]]'s master [[Honen]] and [[Prince Shotoku]]. Due to renovations, the interior of the ''Amidado'' was not accessible to the public until February 2019, when the front half of the hall was opened. The back half was closed until the end of March 2022, when the restoration work concluded. == See also == * [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (miscellaneous structures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Footnotes== :A.{{Note|A}}The defunct ''[[hanamachi]]'' courtesan's district of [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] is located directly to the west of the north side of Nishi Hongan-ji along Hanayachō-dōri. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category|Nishi Hongwanji|Nishi Hongan-ji}} {{Refbegin}} * Kirby, John B.
